claims.claim: Two independent hyperscalers had funded large data-center and AI infrastructure programs by the cutoff: Microsoft disclosed $35.4 billion of construction commitments primarily related to data centers at June 30, 2024, while Alphabet disclosed $38.3 billion of capital expenditures in the first nine months of 2024, primarily technical infrastructure that included AI and data-center construction.

sources.publisher: Microsoft Corporation / U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission
sources.excerpt_or_locator: Form 10-K, Part II Item 7 and Note 7; construction commitments and data-center capital expenditure discussion
sources.limitations: The commitment covers data-center construction broadly and does not identify transformer suppliers or transformer content.

sources.publisher: Alphabet Inc. / U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission
sources.excerpt_or_locator: Form 10-Q, Capital Expenditures and Leases, lines 1918-1929
sources.limitations: Technical infrastructure combines servers, networking, land, and buildings; it does not disclose transformer procurement.

claims.contradiction_search: Checked whether the disclosed spend was only aspirational. Both filings report commitments or incurred capital expenditure, but neither establishes transformer-level spend.
claims.notes: This proves funded downstream demand, not demand captured by any transformer manufacturer.

claims.claim: Independent official assessments available by the cutoff treated U.S. data-center electricity load as material and rising, while also documenting substantial forecast and operating uncertainty for large loads.

sources.excerpt_or_locator: DOE release for the 2024 U.S. Data Center Energy Usage Report; 2023 use and 2028 scenario range
sources.limitations: The 2028 range is a scenario forecast, not contracted load, and covers all data centers rather than AI alone.

sources.excerpt_or_locator: 2024 Long-Term Reliability Assessment, Emerging Issues: Data Centers and Large Industrial Load
sources.limitations: NERC highlights planning and operating uncertainty and does not translate projected load into transformer orders.

claims.contradiction_search: Retained the wide DOE scenario range and NERC warning that large-load timing, behavior, and forecasting are uncertain; no announced project count was treated as committed load.
claims.notes: The claim is intentionally limited to materiality and direction, not a precise AI-only forecast.

claims.claim: Transformer supply constraints existed before the cutoff: NREL reported distribution-transformer lead times of up to two years and DOE identified grain-oriented electrical steel and large-power-transformer manufacturing as weak links.

sources.limitations: The lead-time evidence concerns distribution transformers and cannot be transferred mechanically to large power transformers.

sources.excerpt_or_locator: Electric Grid Supply Chain Deep Dive Assessment, sections 2.1 and 3.2.3
sources.limitations: The assessment predates the 2024 AI demand acceleration and mixes qualitative stakeholder evidence with cited public data.

claims.contradiction_search: Separated distribution transformers from large power transformers and searched for supply additions; C-004 records announced competing capacity.
claims.notes: This supports a real constraint at multiple transformer layers but not a single fungible transformer market.

claims.claim: Large incumbents had already announced material supply responses by the cutoff: Siemens Energy planned a $150 million U.S. power-transformer factory for first output in 2026, and Hitachi Energy planned more than $1.5 billion of global transformer-capacity investment through 2027.

sources.excerpt_or_locator: Press release: Charlotte factory investment, employment, and first-production timing
sources.limitations: Forward plan from a competitor; commissioning and realized output were not yet observed at the cutoff.

sources.excerpt_or_locator: Press release: global capacity program and 2027 timing
sources.limitations: Forward plan from a competitor; capacity mix and eventual utilization were not disclosed.

claims.contradiction_search: Searched specifically for announced incumbent capacity, new factories, and earlier relief dates instead of extrapolating current lead times unchanged.
claims.notes: The capacity response is disconfirming evidence against a perpetual scarcity thesis.

claims.claim: GE Vernova was the NYSE-listed GEV legal entity by the cutoff, and its reported Electrification segment included Grid Solutions together with power conversion, software, and solar and storage technologies.

sources.publisher: GE Vernova Inc. / U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission
sources.excerpt_or_locator: Form 10-Q, About GE Vernova and Note 1; segment composition, legal entity, exchange, and ticker
sources.limitations: The segment aggregates several businesses and does not report transformer-only economics.

claims.notes: Entity and segment relevance are established; exposure purity is not.

claims.claim: For 2024 Q3, GE Vernova reported Electrification orders of $2.5 billion, revenue of $1.9 billion, a 10.4% segment EBITDA margin, and a $5.6 billion increase in Electrification remaining performance obligations from year-end 2023.

sources.publisher: GE Vernova Inc. / U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission
sources.excerpt_or_locator: Form 10-Q, Results of Operations, Electrification and RPO tables
sources.limitations: Orders, revenue, margin, and RPO cover the whole Electrification segment rather than transformers or AI data centers.

claims.notes: These figures cannot be used as transformer-only unit economics.

claims.claim: At its December 2024 investor update, GE Vernova said its Power Transmission business planned to double transformer and switchgear capacity over four years and expected 75% of that growth not to require capital expenditure.

sources.limitations: Management forecast combining transformers and switchgear; no product-level base capacity, revenue, margin, or completed output was disclosed.

claims.notes: The statement supports a capacity plan, not realized transformer rent capture.

claims.claim: Eaton's NYSE-listed ETN legal entity reported data centers and utilities as end markets for its broad Electrical businesses, making it a comparison proxy rather than verified pure transformer exposure.

sources.publisher: Eaton Corporation plc / U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission
sources.excerpt_or_locator: Form 10-K, Item 1 and Note 18; ticker, products, segments, and end markets
sources.limitations: The filing does not disclose AI-data-center transformer revenue separately.

claims.notes: ETN is used only to expose causal overlap in the research comparison set.

claims.claim: The historical theme contains at least two non-fungible constraint layers: campus and distribution transformers, and utility or transmission large power transformers; a security map that merges them would overstate exposure.
claims.contradiction_search: Compared the NREL distribution-transformer definitions with the DOE large-power-transformer supply-chain sections.
claims.notes: This inference is a category-control result, not an estimate of either market's size.

claims.claim: GEV was a relevant listed owner and capacity unlocker, but the cutoff evidence did not isolate transformer revenue, margins, qualified capacity, AI-data-center customer content, or incremental free cash flow; therefore a material transformer-to-per-share bridge remained unverified.
claims.contradiction_search: Reviewed the Q3 filing and December investor materials for transformer-only revenue, margin, capacity, customer, capex, working-capital, and diluted-share disclosures; none completed the bridge.
claims.notes: This unresolved critical inference activates the no-material-revenue-bridge hard flag.

claims.claim: A $325 per-share reference anchor is used solely as an explicit sensitivity assumption for scenario arithmetic.
claims.notes: No cutoff-dated Tier A closing-price record was admitted to this case. The anchor must not be represented as the actual 2024-12-31 closing price.

claims.claim: The modeled scarcity P10/P50/P90 is 12/30/48 months, company monetization lag is 18 months, and market discovery is 6 months.
claims.notes: Research ranges derived from the observed lead-time evidence and announced 2026-2027 capacity additions; they are not calibrated probabilities or company guidance.

claims.claim: Supply expansion could begin relieving the constraint before GEV's transformer-specific per-share monetization is independently established.
claims.contradiction_search: Preserved both the current shortage evidence and dated incumbent capacity plans; neither was allowed to erase the other.
claims.notes: This is the capital-cycle countercase and a reason not to extrapolate the shortage indefinitely.

claims.claim: The evidence supports a real transformer constraint but does not support a verified GEV transformer-to-per-share rent-capture thesis as of the cutoff.
claims.contradiction_search: Tested the strongest positive case from funded demand, backlog, pricing, and capacity against segment aggregation, category mismatch, valuation uncertainty, and competing capacity.
claims.notes: This is the basis for BOTTLENECK_NOT_EQUITY, not a claim that GEV was unattractive for every other reason.
